steps.title: Identify what the passage says about Knight
steps.content: The passage states that Knight, along with other acolytes, imitated Hair's fast painting technique. The passage also says that imitating this technique "accounts in part for the impressionistic qualities that are now synonymous with the group's shared aesthetic." Reagan, by contrast, used greater attention to detail — meaning he did not fully embrace the fast technique.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
Work through all four choices:
  • Choice A — 'Because of the manner in which they were created, they likely have visual qualities that are regarded as more typical of Florida Highwaymen paintings than the qualities in works by Reagan are.': Knight used fast painting, which produces the impressionistic qualities synonymous with the group's aesthetic. Reagan did not fully embrace this approach. So Knight's work more closely reflects the signature group style. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Although it is evident that Knight adopted some of Hair's preferred techniques, Knight's works are less derivative of works by Hair than is typically acknowledged.': The passage says Knight imitated Hair's technique; it says nothing about this being less derivative than commonly thought. This invents a comparison the text never makes. ✗
  • Choice C — 'The lack of precision with which they were executed suggests that they are inferior to works by either Hair or Reagan.': The passage makes no quality judgment — it never calls any painter's work inferior. Fast painting is the group's characteristic approach, not a flaw. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Knight's reliance on the technique of fast painting likely accounts for his works being more aesthetically interesting than works by Reagan are.': "More aesthetically interesting" is a value judgment the passage never makes. The passage describes the style, not which works are more interesting. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
